does: This paper introduces COALA, a lightweight preference learning algorithm that reformulates LLM alignment as a convex optimization problem to enable efficient training on single GPUs. Unlike RLHF which requires a separate reward model and reinforcement learning pipeline, COALA eliminates the need for a reference model entirely by leveraging convex optimization theory applied to neural networks. The method is evaluated across four datasets and six language models including Llama-3.1-8B, demonstrating competitive performance with Direct Preference Optimization while achieving approximately 17.6% of DPO's computational requirements in terms of VRAM and training time.
connects: This work builds directly on recent developments in simplified preference learning, particularly responding to limitations in Direct Preference Optimization which removed the explicit reward modeling step from traditional RLHF pipelines. COALA extends prior research on convex optimization reformulations of neural network training by applying these theoretical insights to the preference alignment problem. The paper addresses known challenges with DPO including inconsistent ranking accuracy and expensive hyperparameter tuning, while drawing on the broader literature on making LLM alignment more practical and accessible. It connects to earlier work on efficient fine-tuning methods and the theoretical foundations of convex neural network optimization.
enables: By dramatically reducing computational requirements for preference-based alignment, COALA enables researchers and practitioners without access to large GPU clusters to fine-tune and adapt language models. This democratization of alignment techniques could facilitate broader exploration of preference learning across diverse domains and model scales. The convex optimization framework opens theoretical avenues for understanding and improving preference learning guarantees. Future work could extend the approach to multi-objective alignment scenarios, investigate theoretical convergence properties in practical settings, and explore whether convex reformulations can be applied to other aspects of LLM training beyond preference optimization.
